commonProblems.h2: Common Volvo Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Volvo’s transition from legendary 5-cylinder and Ford-era blocks to modern Drive-E turbo and hybrid architectures has introduced specific, predictable failure points. While the Swedish brand remains synonymous with safety and high-mileage durability, early Drive-E timing chain wear, diesel DPF/EGR congestion, and T8 hybrid inverter faults now dominate replacement decisions. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Use this decision tool to compare genuine repair costs versus replacement, and discover when a specialist-fitted reconditioned engine offers better value than a main dealer repair.

problems.h4: Drive-E Timing Chain & Guide Wear – The Early Failure That Kills Volvo’s Modern Petrol & Diesel Engines
problems.affectedModels: Volvo V40, V60, XC60, XC90, S60, S90, V90 (2014–2019, Drive-E 2.0 litre 4-cylinder petrol & diesel: B4204T, B4204T11, B4204T15, D4204T, D4204T5, D4204T14)
problems.rootCause: The plastic timing chain guides and tensioner system on early Drive-E units degrade faster than expected, causing chain slack. When the guides crack or the tensioner loses hydraulic pressure, the chain can skip teeth on the camshaft sprockets, leading to poor running, rattle on cold start, and eventual catastrophic valve-to-piston contact.
problems.recommendation: If your Drive-E engine rattles for more than two seconds on cold start or logs camshaft correlation faults (P0016/P0017), book a specialist inspection immediately. A preventative chain replacement at a Volvo specialist saves you £1,000+ over main dealer pricing and typically prevents engine death. However, if the chain has already jumped, a full rebuilt engine with upgraded timing components costs significantly less than a main dealer short block — and includes a superior warranty.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Drive-E engine prices for your V60, XC60 or XC90

problems.h4: D5 & D4204T Turbo Actuator Failure – The Power Loss Nightmare That Leads to Replacement
problems.affectedModels: Volvo XC60, XC70, V70, S80, V60, S60 (2008–2016, 5-cylinder D5244T, 4-cylinder D4204T, D4204T5)
problems.rootCause: The variable geometry turbo actuator (electronic or vacuum-controlled) seizes due to carbon buildup or electrical failure. When the actuator stops responding, the turbo either overboosts or underboosts, triggering limp mode, excessive smoke, and eventual turbocharger overspeed damage.
problems.recommendation: On a D5 or D4 Volvo worth £3,000–£7,000, a standalone actuator replacement at a Volvo diesel specialist is excellent value — but only if caught immediately. If you’ve driven the car in limp mode for more than 50 miles, upgrade to the cartridge replacement. If the turbo has fully grenaded, do not cut corners: fit a rebuilt engine with a new turbo to avoid repeat failures and oil contamination issues.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for D5 turbo repair or replacement engines for your XC60 or V70

problems.h4: DPF & EGR Congestion – The Diesel Daily Driver’s Silent Killer
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 70,000 – 110,000 miles (worse for low-mileage or short-journey cars)
problems.rootCause: Diesel Particulate Filters (DPF) and Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) systems clog with soot and ash when Volvos are used predominantly for short trips without regular regeneration cycles. A blocked DPF increases exhaust back pressure, while EGR buildup reduces airflow — together they cause poor running, failed MOTs on emissions, and eventual turbo or piston ring damage.
problems.recommendation: If your Volvo diesel regularly undertakes short journeys, book a specialist forced regen and EGR clean every 20,000 miles — it’s the cheapest insurance against replacement. If you already see DPF warning lights and the car won’t regen, replace the DPF and EGR together. On a high-mileage car with compression loss or oil contamination, switch to a rebuilt engine with a new DPF fitted. The main dealer premium for these items is rarely justified.
problems.cta: → Compare DPF cleaning vs engine replacement costs for your Volvo diesel

problems.h4: 5-Cylinder Crankshaft Pulley / Harmonic Balancer Failure – The Overlooked Catastrophe
problems.rootCause: The rubber bonding in the crankshaft pulley (harmonic balancer) degrades and separates. When it fails completely, the pulley can detach, shred auxiliary belts, and — critically — allow the timing belt to jump or snap, causing immediate valve and piston collision.
problems.recommendation: On any 5-cylinder Volvo approaching 90,000 miles, replace the crank pulley as preventative maintenance — even if it looks fine. A £250 specialist preventative job saves you a £2,000+ top-end rebuild or a £3,000+ replacement engine. If the pulley has already failed and you only have bent valves, a top-end rebuild at a quality specialist is viable. But if there’s lower-end noise or the car has been cranked extensively post-failure, move straight to a rebuilt engine.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 5-cylinder Volvo engine prices with upgraded pulleys

problems.group: T8 Twin Engine PHEV High-Voltage Inverter Integration Fault
problems.h4: T8 Twin Engine PHEV High-Voltage Inverter Integration Fault – The Hybrid-Only Nightmare
problems.affectedModels: Volvo XC90 T8, XC60 T8, S90 T8, V90 T8 (2016–2020, B4204T35 + ERAD electric rear axle drive)
problems.rootCause: The high-voltage inverter (converting battery DC to AC for the electric motor) and the ERAD (Electric Rear Axle Drive) unit suffer from software integration faults and coolant ingress. When the inverter fails, the hybrid system is disabled, the car loses electric drive, and in some cases the combustion engine will not start due to CAN bus communication errors.
problems.recommendation: On a T8 Volvo with hybrid system warnings, start with a specialist software update and full diagnostic scan — main dealers often jump straight to inverter replacement. If the inverter has failed, a reconditioned unit fitted by a hybrid specialist costs 40% less than a main dealer and includes the same warranty. However, if coolant has entered the ERAD electric motor, the entire rear drive assembly must be replaced; do not accept an inverter-only fix in that case.
problems.cta: → Compare T8 hybrid inverter & ERAD replacement costs for your XC90 or XC60

problems.h4: Oil Consumption & Piston Ring Seizure – The Drive-E Petrol Engine Killer
problems.rootCause: Low-friction piston rings on early Drive-E petrol engines become clogged with carbon, losing tension and allowing oil to bypass into the combustion chamber. Owners first notice oil consumption of 1 litre per 1,000 miles, followed by catalytic converter damage, misfires, and eventual piston scoring.
problems.recommendation: If your Drive-E petrol uses less than 0.5L of oil per 1,000 miles, a specialist chemical carbon clean can buy you 20,000–30,000 miles. But once consumption exceeds 1L per 1,000 miles, the rings are already seized. At that point, do not pay main dealer prices for a piston ring replacement — a fully rebuilt engine with upgraded rings costs roughly the same and includes a 12-month unlimited mileage warranty, offering far better long-term security.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Drive-E petrol engine prices for your V60 or XC60

engineTypes.h2: Volvo Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Volvo engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle’s age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Volvo Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the main dealer price.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £2,200 – £4,800 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Volvo Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as upgraded timing chains for Drive-E units and reinforced turbo actuators for D5 engines – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Volvo Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.title: Volvo Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for T8 Twin Engine models or owners wanting a zero‑mile unit.

types.title: Volvo Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn’t had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older V70 or S80 models where a full recon isn’t economical.

types.title: Volvo Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Volvo specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £500 – £900 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Volvo engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Volvo model today.

engineSizes.h2: Volvo Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Volvo engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Volvo 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6-litre four-cylinder diesel engine joined the Volvo range in 2010, badged D2 . This PSA-derived unit (DV6C / D4162T) was fitted to the C30, S40, V50 and C70 as an ultra-economy option, delivering CO2 emissions as low as 114g/km and excellent fuel economy . While efficient, these units are known for clogged DPFs when used exclusively for short journeys, alongside EGR valve and turbocharger failures. Engine code(s): D4162T, DV6C Compatible models (UK): C30, S40, V50, C70 (2010–2013) Production years: 2010–2013 Common failure points: DPF clogging, EGR valve sticking, turbocharger failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Volvo 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Volvo 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Volvo’s 2.0-litre diesel range spans multiple generations, from the five-cylinder D3/D4 units introduced in 2010 to the revolutionary four-cylinder Drive-E diesel family that followed . The five-cylinder 2.0D (D3/D4) appeared across the S80, V70, XC70, XC60, S60, V60, C30, S40 and V50, while the later Drive-E D3 and D4 units (D4204T family) powered the V40, V60, XC60, XC90, S60, S90, V90 and XC40 . These engines are generally reliable at high mileage but suffer from timing chain guide wear on early Drive-E units, DPF/EGR congestion on short journeys, and turbo actuator failure on D5/D4 variants. Engine code(s): D4204T, D4204T5, D4204T14, five-cylinder 2.0D (D3/D4) Compatible models (UK): V40, V60, XC60, XC90, S60, S90, V90, XC40, S80, V70, XC70, C30, S40, V50, C70 Production years: 2010–present Common failure points: Drive-E timing chain guide wear, DPF/EGR blockage, turbo actuator failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Volvo 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Volvo 2.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The legendary 2.4-litre five-cylinder D5 diesel is one of Volvo’s most iconic powerplants, known for its distinctive warble and exceptional longevity when maintained properly. Introduced in the early 2000s and refined through multiple generations (including the 163bhp, 185bhp and 215bhp twin-turbo versions), the D5 appeared across nearly every Volvo model . The naturally-aspirated 2.4D version was also widely available. Early D5 units suffer from injector failure and throttle body issues, while later twin-turbo variants can experience turbo actuator and swirl flap problems. The 2.4-litre D24 six-cylinder inline diesel powered the classic 200 and 700 Series estates . Engine code(s): D5244T, D5244T4, D5244T5, D5244T8, D24 Compatible models (UK): V70, XC70, XC90, S60, S80, V60, XC60, 200 Series, 700 Series, 900 Series Production years: 1990–2015 Common failure points: Injector failure, throttle body issues (early D5), turbo actuator (twin-turbo), harmonic balancer separation
items.cta: Get quotes for Volvo 2.4L diesel engines

items.title: Volvo 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre three-cylinder Drive-E petrol engine arrived with the 2016 model year updates, badged T2 and T3 . This compact turbocharged unit was developed as part of Volvo’s modular Drive-E family, fitted primarily to the V40 and V40 Cross Country. In T3 form, it produced 150bhp while returning up to 51.4mpg . These engines are generally robust, but like all direct-injection units, they can suffer from intake valve carbon buildup. Early examples also show occasional oil consumption concerns linked to piston ring design. Engine code(s): B4154T Compatible models (UK): V40, V40 Cross Country (2016–2019) Production years: 2016–2019 Common failure points: Carbon buildup on intake valves, piston ring oil consumption
items.cta: Compare Volvo 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volvo 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrol engine is the cornerstone of Volvo’s modern Drive-E family, available in naturally aspirated, turbocharged (T4, T5), and turbocharged-and-supercharged (T6, T8 Twin Engine) forms . The B4204T family powers virtually every current and recent Volvo model: V40, V60, XC60, XC90, S60, S90, V90 and XC40 . The T8 Twin Engine plug-in hybrid variants pair the 2.0-litre petrol unit with an electric motor for combined outputs up to 449bhp . Earlier non-Drive-E 2.0-litre petrol engines (B20, B19, B200) powered classic models including the 240, 740, 940 and early 850 . The B4204T family’s weakness is early timing chain guide wear and oil consumption through carbon-clogged piston rings, while T6 variants have additional supercharger clutch and cooling system complexity. Engine code(s): B4204T, B4204T11, B4204T15, B4204T35 (T8), B20, B19/B200 Compatible models (UK): V40, V60, XC60, XC90, S60, S90, V90, XC40, 240, 740, 940, 850 Production years: 1970–present Common failure points: Drive-E timing chain guide wear, piston ring oil consumption, supercharger clutch (T6)
items.cta: Compare Volvo 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volvo 2.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The iconic 2.3-litre five-cylinder turbocharged engine (B5234T) powered the legendary Volvo 850 T-5 and T-5R, as well as early S70, V70, C70 and S60 models. This engine transformed Volvo’s reputation for performance, producing up to 250bhp from the factory while retaining five-cylinder character and genuine estate-car practicality . The naturally-aspirated B230 engine powered the 240, 740 and 940 for decades with bulletproof simplicity. Turbo versions are known for PCV system clogging causing oil seal blowouts, and original factory turbocharger failure around 120,000-150,000 miles. Engine code(s): B5234T, B230F, B230FT Compatible models (UK): 850 T-5/T-5R, S70, V70, C70, S60, 240, 740, 940 Production years: 1985–2000 Common failure points: PCV system clogging, factory turbo wear, wiring harness degradation (classic models)
items.cta: Compare Volvo 2.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volvo 2.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.5-litre five-cylinder petrol engine (B5254S) powered early 850 models, while turbocharged versions appeared in the 850 TDI and later V70, S60 and C70 models. The 2.5-litre T5 variants produced up to 250bhp with the characteristic five-cylinder soundtrack . These engines share the same PCV and turbo concerns as the 2.3-litre family, with an additional vulnerability around the Electronic Throttle Module (ETM) on earlier electronic throttle cars. The B5254T2 and later T5 variants are more robust, though all five-cylinder engines rely on a healthy PCV system to avoid rear main seal failure. Engine code(s): B5254S, B5254T, B5254T2, B5254T5 Compatible models (UK): 850, S70, V70, C70, S60, S80 Production years: 1992–2009 Common failure points: PCV system clogging, ETM failure (early cars), turbo wear
items.cta: Compare Volvo 2.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volvo 2.9L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.9-litre straight-six petrol engine (B6294S) powered the flagship S80 and S90 models, offering silky refinement and 200+bhp from a naturally aspirated six-cylinder layout . A twin-turbo version (B6294T) appeared in the S80 T6, producing 268bhp. These engines are exceptionally smooth and torquey but have a notorious weak point: the GM-sourced automatic transmission (4T65EV/GT) fitted to T6 models fails frequently under the engine’s 380Nm torque loading, leading to expensive transmission replacements. The straight-six layout is longitudinally mounted and therefore physically large, making in-situ repairs more labour intensive. Engine code(s): B6304S, B6294S, B6294T Compatible models (UK): S80, S90, 960 Production years: 1995–2005 Common failure points: Transmission failure (T6 only), timing belt interference engine (regular replacement essential)
items.cta: Compare Volvo 2.9L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volvo 3.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre straight-six turbocharged T6 petrol engine represents the zenith of Volvo’s pre-Drive-E six-cylinder era. Delivering 298bhp and 324lb ft of torque, the T6 appeared in the S80, V70, XC60 and XC90 . This engine is silky smooth and powerful, but suffers auxiliary and supercharger belt failures if not diligently maintained, alongside PCV system issues common to all Volvo six-cylinders. The T6’s tight engine bay also makes repairs labour-intensive, and the six-cylinder block was eventually phased out in favour of the smaller, lighter Drive-E four-cylinder family. Engine code(s): B6304T Compatible models (UK): S80, V70, XC60, XC90 Production years: 2007–2015 Common failure points: Belt failures (auxiliary/supercharger), PCV system clogging
items.cta: Compare Volvo 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volvo 2.0L Hybrid Engines (T8 Twin Engine)
items.description: Volvo’s T8 Twin Engine plug-in hybrid powertrain combines a turbocharged and supercharged 2.0-litre Drive-E petrol engine with an 87hp electric motor driving the rear axle . Available across the XC90, XC60, S90, V90 and S60 ranges, the T8 produces combined outputs from 390hp up to 455hp with Polestar Engineered versions . The electric motor provides up to 36 miles of zero-emission range, while the petrol engine handles longer journeys . These complex systems suffer high-voltage inverter integration faults and ERAD (Electric Rear Axle Drive) coolant ingress issues that can disable the hybrid system. Repair costs are significant, with inverter replacement typically required. System / motor code(s): B4204T35 (petrol), ERAD (electric) Compatible models (UK): XC90 T8, XC60 T8, S90 T8, V90 T8, S60 T8 Production years: 2016–present Important notes: Inverter failure common; ERAD coolant ingress destroys electric motor

engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Volvo engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Volvo specialists.

entries.title: D5244T4 – 2.4L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the Volvo S60, V70, and XC90 between 2005–2009 (185hp). This 5-cylinder D5 is known for turbo actuator seizure (causing overboost limp mode) and injector wear that leads to poor cold starting and increased diesel knock. A quality rebuilt unit includes a professionally reconditioned VNT turbo actuator, tested and calibrated injectors, a new timing belt kit, and a full gasket set.
entries.cta: Get quotes for D5244T4 Volvo engine replacement

entries.title: D5244T5 – 2.4L Diesel
entries.description: Found in the Volvo XC90, V70, and S80 (2006–2011, 185hp). Shares the same D5 architecture and identical failure points as the D5244T4: turbo actuator failure, injector wear, and EGR clogging on higher-mileage examples. Rebuilt units receive upgraded injector seals, a reconditioned turbo, and a new timing belt with water pump.

entries.title: D5244T16 / D5244T17 – 2.4L Twin-Turbo Diesel
entries.description: Powering the Volvo XC70, XC60, and S80 (2008–2015, 215hp twin-turbo). The higher-output twin-turbo D5 suffers from the same actuator issues as the single-turbo version but adds complexity with two VNT mechanisms. Failed swirl flaps and carbon build-up in the intake manifold are also common. Rebuilt units include both turbos reconditioned, a cleaned intake manifold, and upgraded injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for D5244T16 / D5244T17 Volvo engine replacement

entries.title: D4204T – 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Fitted to the Volvo XC60, S60, V60, and V70 (2010–2015, 163hp). This early Drive-E (VEA) diesel is known for timing chain stretch (causing rattle and eventual jumping) and EGR valve clogging that leads to restricted power and limp mode. Rebuilt units include an upgraded timing chain kit, a fully cleaned EGR system, and a new DPF where required.

entries.title: D4204T8 – 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Found in the Volvo XC60, S90, V90, and XC90 (2015–2019, 181hp). This higher-output Drive-E diesel suffers from the same timing chain weakness as the D4204T but also experiences crankcase pressure issues from blocked PCV systems. A professionally rebuilt unit includes an upgraded timing chain set, a new PCV/oil separator, and a full engine gasket kit.
entries.cta: Get quotes for D4204T8 Volvo engine replacement

entries.title: D4204T14 – 2.0L Diesel
entries.description: Powering the Volvo XC60, S90, V90 (2016–2020, 150hp). This lower-output variant of the Drive-E diesel exhibits timing chain stretch later (typically 80,000–120,000 miles) but is also prone to EGR cooler cracking and coolant loss. Rebuilt units include timing components, a replacement EGR cooler (where required), and a new water pump.

entries.title: B4204T – 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to the Volvo XC60, S/V60, and S/V90 (2014–2019, 245hp). This turbocharged Drive-E petrol engine suffers from timing chain stretch and pulley wear, with some examples also experiencing piston ring issues causing oil consumption. Rebuilt units include an upgraded timing chain kit, new rings where required, and a revised oil pump assembly.
entries.cta: Get quotes for B4204T Volvo engine replacement

entries.title: B4204T11 – 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: Found in the Volvo XC90, S90, and V90 (2015–2019, 190hp). The lower-output version of the B4204T family has fewer piston ring problems but still experiences timing chain wear and VVT hub failure. A quality rebuilt unit includes replacement VVT hubs, an upgraded timing chain, and a full gasket set with new head bolts.

entries.title: B4204T27 – 2.0L Petrol
entries.description: (T5) Powering the Volvo XC60, XC90, S90, and V90 (2016–2021, 310hp). This high-output turbo-only T5 variant (without supercharger) suffers from the same timing chain wear but adds boost-related stress on the PCV system. Rebuilt units include upgraded timing components, a new PCV/oil separator, and a reconditioned turbocharger.
entries.cta: Get quotes for B4204T27 Volvo engine replacement

families.name: Volvo Early Petrol Turbo (B5254T / B4164T Families) (2005–2015)

entries.title: B5254T7 – 2.5L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to the Volvo V50, C70, and S40 (2005–2010, 230hp). This 5-cylinder turbo (derived from the earlier whiteblock) is known for PCV system clogging (causing rear main seal oil leaks) and timing belt service neglect. Rebuilt units include a new PCV kit with oil trap, fresh timing belt and tensioner, and a reconditioned turbo.

entries.title: B5254T12 – 2.5L Petrol
entries.description: (T5) Found in the Volvo C30, S40, and V50 (2007–2011, 254hp). This higher-output T5 shares the same PCV and timing belt vulnerabilities as the B5254T7 but adds cooling system stress on track or hard-driven examples. Quality rebuilt engines include an upgraded PCV system, new water pump, and a full cooling system check.
entries.cta: Get quotes for B5254T12 Volvo engine replacement

entries.title: B4164T2 – 1.6L Petrol
entries.description: Powering the Volvo S60, V60, V70, S80, and C30 (2010–2015, 180hp). This Ford-sourced 1.6L EcoBoost turbo suffers from coolant leakage from the exhaust manifold area and oil seepage from the vacuum pump. A rebuilt unit includes a new manifold gasket, a reconditioned vacuum pump, and a fresh timing belt.

entries.title: B8444S – 4.4L Petrol
entries.description: Fitted to the Volvo XC90 and S80 (2005–2011, 315hp). This Yamaha-designed V8 is reliable in most respects but suffers from valve train wear on early models, expensive timing components (chains and guides), and some reports of block porosity in high-mileage units. Rebuilt units include upgraded valve gear, new timing chain kits, and full block inspection for porosity. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? engineYears.h2: Volvo Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When

years.preview: Five-Cylinder Modular & Early TDI Foundation 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Volvo's late-1990s range carried the brand's definitive inline five-cylinder petrol and diesel families across the S40, S60, S70, V70, S80, and XC series - engines built on a modular architecture that gave every displacement variant high parts interchangeability and laid the groundwork for over a decade of UK replacement demand. Key Changes B5244S / B5244T (2.4L I5 petrol) across S60, V70, S80 - naturally aspirated and turbocharged variants confirmed in UK production B6294S / B6294T (2.9L I6 petrol) in S80 flagship - six-cylinder for the premium executive segment D5244T (2.4L I5 diesel) introduced from 2001 on S60, V70, S80, and XC70 - first modern Volvo five-cylinder common-rail diesel T5 2.3L B5234T turbo continues in S40/V40 as performance flagship Ford-era platform integration (P1) begins in S40/V40 - Ford Duratorq diesel 2.0 TDi (D4204T based development begins) Main Engines B5244S / B5244S2 - 2.4L I5 naturally aspirated petrol 140hp (Volvo S60, V70, S80) B5244T / B5244T3 - 2.4L I5 turbocharged petrol 200hp (Volvo S60, S80, V70 T5) B6294S / B6294T - 2.9L I6 petrol 200–272hp (Volvo S80 T6, C70) D5244T - 2.4L I5 common-rail diesel 163hp (Volvo S60 D5, V70, S80, XC70) B5234T - 2.3L I5 turbo petrol (Volvo S40/V40 T5) Popular Models Volvo S60 (2001 first gen) Volvo V70 (P26) Volvo S80 (P28 first gen) Volvo XC70 Volvo S40/V40 (P1 early) Known For D5244T five-cylinder diesel is widely regarded as one of the most characterful and robust diesel engines of the early 2000s - the inline-five soundtrack and smooth torque delivery earn near-universal praise from UK owners Early D5244T injector wear and fuel system sensitivity are documented at 120,000+ miles - hard cold starts and increased smoke are the first signs, and replacement injectors remain widely available B5244S naturally aspirated five-cylinder is simple, long-lived, and oil-maintenance-friendly - UK specialists regard it as among the lowest-risk used Volvo petrol purchases of the era Timing belt on D5244T requires disciplined 96,000-mile or 10-year replacement - belt-driven water pump means a skipped service is catastrophic; a non-negotiable pre-purchase check on any used example [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: B5244S, B5244S2, B5244T, B5244T3, B5234T, B6294S, B6294T, D5244T
years.cta: Compare 1998–2001 Volvo five-cylinder petrol and diesel replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: S40 Mk2/V50, XC90 T6 & D5 High Output 🔴 Performance Landmark
years.description: Short intro: The new P1 platform S40 and V50 brought a generation-defining combination of Ford-derived four-cylinder engines and Volvo's own turbocharged five-cylinder into the compact segment, while the first-generation XC90 launched with the supercharged T6 inline-six - Volvo's most powerful production engine at the time and a performance landmark for the brand. Key Changes New S40 Mk2 / V50 (P1 platform, 2004) - B4184S / B5254T2 / D4164T introduced, sharing platform with Ford Focus/C-Max XC90 T6 (2003) - B6304T 2.9L supercharged I6 286hp; first Volvo SUV with genuine performance credentials in UK D5244T4 (2.4L I5 diesel 185hp) introduced on S60, V70, and XC90 D5 from 2005 - power increase, swirl flap actuator added D4164T (1.6L four-cylinder diesel) introduced on S40/V50 from 2005 - Ford-sourced unit B5254T2 (2.5L I5 turbo 210hp) in S60 T5 and V70 T5 - replaces 2.3L unit, enlarged capacity with improved low-end torque Main Engines B5254T2 / B5254T4 - 2.5L I5 turbo petrol 210–220hp (Volvo S60, V70, S80, XC90) B6304T - 2.9L I6 supercharged petrol 272–286hp (Volvo XC90 T6, S80 T6) D5244T4 - 2.4L I5 diesel 185hp high output (Volvo S60, V70, XC70, XC90 D5) D4164T - 1.6L I4 diesel 109hp (Volvo S40, V50) B4184S - 1.8L I4 petrol 125hp (Volvo S40, V50 entry) Popular Models Volvo XC90 T6 (P28) Volvo S60 T5 / D5 Volvo V70 T5 / D5 Volvo S40 Mk2 (P1) Volvo V50 (P1) Known For XC90 T6's supercharged B6304T is a prestige-oriented unit - smooth and potent, but supercharger oil seal leaks and timing chain tensioner wear at 100,000+ miles are the primary UK specialist concerns D5244T4 185hp introduces swirl flaps - a well-documented failure point where broken flap debris enters the engine and causes catastrophic damage; UK specialists universally recommend removal and blanking at inspection B5254T2 2.5T is celebrated for its linear turbocharged torque delivery and strong durability - one of the most trusted performance Volvo petrols in the UK used-car market Ford-sourced D4164T in S40/V50 is an adequate economy unit but lacks the character of Volvo's own diesels - DPF blockage in urban UK use is the most common workshop complaint on this model pairing [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: B5254T2, B5254T3, B5254T4, B6304T, D5244T4, D4164T, B4184S11, B4204S3
years.cta: Find 2002–2006 Volvo XC90, S60, and V70 five-cylinder replacement engines with UK warranty

years.preview: C30/C70 Launch, D5 215hp & Polestar Era Begins
years.description: Short intro: This period saw Volvo extend the five-cylinder modular family to its highest performance expression - the 215hp D5244T15 diesel and 230hp+ B5254T petrol variants - while the C30 coupe and C70 Mk2 cabriolet brought the engine range into new lifestyle body styles that commanded strong UK buyer interest. Key Changes C30 (2006) and C70 Mk2 (2006) launched on P1 platform - B4204S3, B5254T2, and D4164T/D4204T engines D5244T15 (2.4L I5 diesel 215hp) introduced from 2010 on V60, S80, XC60 - highest-output Volvo five-cylinder diesel XC60 Mk1 (2008) launched - B6304T4 3.0L T6 and D5 five-cylinder engine range; first compact Volvo SUV B6304T2 / B6304T4 (2.9L/3.0L I6) updated on S80 and XC60 T6 - revised output, improved compression Polestar performance software programme established in the UK - applied to B5254T and D5244T variants, improving calibration within factory tolerances Main Engines D5244T15 - 2.4L I5 diesel 215hp (Volvo S80, XC60, XC70, V60 from 2010) D5244T4 - 2.4L I5 diesel 185hp (Volvo S60, V70, XC90 continuing) B5254T6 - 2.5L I5 turbo petrol 230hp (Volvo C30, S40, V50 T5) B6304T4 - 2.9L I6 turbo petrol 304hp (Volvo XC60 T6, S80 T6) D4204T - 2.0L I4 diesel 136hp (Volvo S40, C30, V50) Popular Models Volvo XC60 Mk1 (2008–2010) Volvo C30 (P1) Volvo S80 T6 / D5 Volvo V60 Mk1 (early) Volvo XC70 D5 Known For D5244T15 215hp is widely praised as the performance high-water mark of Volvo's five-cylinder diesel - strong mid-range torque and EU motorway-capable fuel economy, with EGR and DPF maintenance the primary running cost D4204T in S40/C30 suffers from EGR cooler failure and associated coolant contamination - an emerging issue at 100,000 miles that is a growing UK specialist concern Timing belt discipline on the five-cylinder diesel family remains a non-negotiable - specialists note that many UK V60 and XC60 examples arrive at 80,000 miles without confirmed belt history, making replacement the first action Polestar tuned variants generate above-average replacement enquiries when high-output use accelerates clutch, DMF, and turbo wear beyond standard service intervals [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: D5244T15, D5244T4, B5254T6, B6304T4, B6304T2, D4204T, B4204S3, B4204T6
years.cta: Browse 2006–2010 Volvo D5 and T6 replacement engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Drive-E Development, Final Five-Cylinders & XC60/V60 Peak 🔴 Major Reliability Revision
years.description: Short intro: This transitional period represented the peak deployment of Volvo's proven five-cylinder engine family across XC60, V60, S60, and XC70 - while simultaneously marking the last years of the modular architecture before the clean-sheet Drive-E four-cylinders took over, with key reliability updates addressing the most significant D5 and T5 known issues. Key Changes D5244T15 updated with revised EGR calibration and improved turbocharger actuator from 2011 build - addressing earlier variable geometry sticking B6324S5 (3.2L I6 petrol) on XC60/XC90/V70 - naturally aspirated six-cylinder for buyers who want refinement without turbo complexity V60 PHEV (T6 Twin Engine) previewed 2012 and launched 2014 - first factory PHEV Volvo; combines B4204T petrol with rear electric motor D5204T (2.0L I5 diesel) fitted to V40 (2012) - downsized five-cylinder for premium compact segment D4204T14 (2.0L I4 diesel - early Drive-E diesel prototype spec) applied to select XC60/S60 from 2013 as Drive-E transition begins Main Engines D5244T15 - 2.4L I5 diesel 215hp (Volvo XC60, S60, V60, XC70 D5) B6324S5 - 3.2L I6 naturally aspirated petrol 243hp (Volvo XC60, XC90, S80) B5254T8 - 2.5L I5 turbo petrol 215hp (Volvo S60, V60, XC60 T5 late) D5204T - 2.0L I5 diesel 136hp (Volvo V40 - first use) B4204T9 - 2.0L I4 petrol Drive-E T5 pre-launch (Volvo S60, XC60 from late 2013) Popular Models Volvo XC60 Mk1 (P3 facelift) Volvo S60 Mk2 (2010–2014) Volvo V60 Mk1 Volvo V40 Mk2 (2012) Volvo XC70 Mk2 (facelift) Known For D5244T15 remains the dominant Volvo diesel replacement search in the UK at this time - swirl flap maintenance, EGR cooler condition, and timing belt age are the three-point pre-purchase checklist for every specialist B6324S naturally aspirated I6 is respected for its smoothness and mechanical simplicity but oil consumption at 120,000+ miles is a widely reported characteristic - valve stem seal wear is the primary cause V40 with D5204T generates consistent diesel replacement enquiries as the compact hatch ages in the used-car market - EGR valve and DPF failures in stop-start UK city commuting are the leading faults V60 Twin Engine PHEV generates specialist interest but its complexity - combining B4204T petrol with rear electric drive - means engine-only replacements require careful pre-diagnosis to isolate the petrol unit from drivetrain electrical faults [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: D5244T15, B6324S5, B5254T8, D5204T, D5204T5, B4204T9, B4204T6, B4204S3
years.cta: Get quotes for 2010–2014 Volvo D5, T5, and 3.2 I6 replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: Drive-E Launch: 2.0L Four-Cylinder Replaces All Five and Six-Cylinders 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Volvo's Drive-E programme was the most ambitious engine programme in the brand's modern history - replacing the entire five and six-cylinder lineup with a single 2.0-litre four-cylinder family across every model, from T2 to T6 petrol and D3 to D5 diesel, marking the definitive end of the inline-five and six era. Key Changes Drive-E 2.0L I4 petrol (B4204T23, B4204T35) replaces all B5254T and B6304T engines - T2 (120hp), T3 (152hp), T4 (180hp), T5 (245hp), T6 (306hp twin-charged) Drive-E 2.0L I4 diesel (D4204T14, D4204T8) replaces D5244T - D3 (150hp), D4 (190hp), D5 (220hp) New XC90 Mk2 (2015) - first full Drive-E platform vehicle with T5, T6, D4, D5 across the range T8 Twin Engine PHEV (B4204T34) introduced on XC90 2015 - 2.0L turbocharged + supercharged petrol plus rear electric motor, 400hp system D4204T14 recalled for intake manifold melting risk on certain 2014–2017 production dates - a significant safety concern requiring urgent dealer check Main Engines B4204T23 / B4204T35 - 2.0L I4 T5/T6 Drive-E petrol 245–306hp (Volvo XC90, S60, V60, XC60) B4204T9 - 2.0L I4 T4 Drive-E petrol 180hp (Volvo V40, S60, V60, XC60) D4204T14 - 2.0L I4 D4/D5 Drive-E diesel 190–220hp (Volvo XC90, S60, XC60) D4204T8 - 2.0L I4 D3 Drive-E diesel 150hp (Volvo S60, V60, V40) B4204T34 - 2.0L I4 T8 PHEV petrol 303hp (Volvo XC90 T8 Recharge) Popular Models Volvo XC90 Mk2 (SPA) Volvo XC60 Mk1 (facelift Drive-E) Volvo S60 Mk2 (Drive-E) Volvo V60 Mk1 (Drive-E) Volvo V40 (Drive-E) Volvo XC90 T8 Recharge Known For D4204T14 intake manifold recall (2014–2019 build) is the most serious safety issue in Drive-E's launch period - the plastic manifold can melt under certain conditions, presenting a fire risk; any unrecalled car must be checked immediately Drive-E D4 EGR cooler sealing failure is a persistent warranty and post-warranty concern - two-chamber cooler design allows exhaust gas to mix with coolant, triggering engine management warning and costly replacement T6 Drive-E twin-charged (turbo + supercharger) in XC90 and S90 is a technically sophisticated unit - supercharger boost at low revs transitioning to turbo at higher revs gives exceptional in-gear response but supercharger coupling wear at 80,000+ miles is an emerging UK specialist concern XC90 T8 PHEV generates strong enthusiast praise for its performance and real-world efficiency on electrified motorway routes - battery degradation and rear electric drive (ERAD) seal failures are the primary ageing concerns [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: B4204T23, B4204T35, B4204T9, D4204T14, D4204T8, D4204T11, D4204T23, B4204T34, B3154T
years.cta: Find 2014–2017 Volvo Drive-E D4 and T5/T6 replacement engines with UK intake manifold recall check

years.description: Short intro: Volvo's Scalable Product Architecture (SPA) platform rolled out across the 90-series and XC40, consolidating Drive-E engines into their most mature and refined expression - while the T8 PHEV expanded to the S90, V90, XC60, and V60 Recharge, making plug-in hybrid the premium choice across virtually every Volvo model. Key Changes S90 / V90 (2016–2017) launched on SPA - Drive-E T4, T5, T6, D4, D5 engines across executive and estate body styles XC40 (CMA platform, 2018) - T3, T4, T5 petrol and D3, D4 diesel Drive-E range; first use of CMA (Compact Modular Architecture) T8 Recharge expands to XC60, V60, S90, V90 - B4204T34/T35/T46 variants across each model D5 220hp quietly retired from XC60 front-wheel drive - D4 190hp becomes dominant diesel across most Volvo models V90 CC (Cross Country) and S90 Ambience carry D5244T (five-cylinder) into 2017 as carry-over on AWD - last production use of the five-cylinder diesel in a new Volvo Main Engines B4204T35 / B4204T45 - 2.0L I4 T5 petrol 250hp (Volvo XC60, S90, V90, XC40) B4204T46 - 2.0L I4 T6 petrol 310hp (Volvo XC60, S90, V90) D4204T11 / D4204T23 - 2.0L I4 D4 diesel 190hp (Volvo XC60, V90, S90, XC40) B4204T28 / B4204T34 / B4204T35 - 2.0L I4 T8 PHEV 318–320hp (Volvo XC60, V60, S90 Recharge) D5244T15 - 2.4L I5 D5 diesel final production carry-over on V90/S90 AWD Popular Models Volvo XC60 Mk2 (SPA) Volvo S90 / V90 (SPA) Volvo V90 Cross Country Volvo XC40 Mk1 (CMA) Volvo XC90 Mk2 (facelift T8) Volvo V60 Mk2 Recharge Known For Drive-E D4 in XC60 Mk2 and V90 continues EGR cooler sealing concern from earlier platform - UK specialists report the issue persisting on post-2017 builds; EGR cooler condition is now a standard first-inspection item on any used Drive-E diesel Volvo T8 Recharge in XC60 Mk2 is near-universally praised for performance and real-world efficiency - ERAD rear motor seal leaks and battery management module faults emerge at 60,000+ miles on early SPA examples XC40 on CMA platform introduces Volvo's compact segment to Drive-E for the first time - T3 three-cylinder B3154T is smooth but sensitive to oil maintenance; timing chain (not belt) marks a change from the five-cylinder diesel heritage Final V90/S90 AWD D5 five-cylinder cars generate collector interest among Volvo enthusiasts - the last new cars sold with the iconic I5 diesel architecture [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: B4204T35, B4204T45, B4204T46, D4204T11, D4204T23, B4204T28, B4204T34, B3154T, D5244T15
years.cta: Get quotes for 2017–2020 Volvo XC60, V90, and XC40 Drive-E replacement engines

years.preview: B-Series 48V Mild Hybrid Launch: B3, B4, B5, B6 🔴 Technology Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Volvo's introduction of the B-series mild hybrid engines from 2020 represented the brand's most significant powertrain transition since Drive-E - the B3, B4, B5, and B6 badges replaced T3, T4, T5, and T6 respectively, with a 48V Belt-integrated Starter Generator (BSG) added to every combustion variant across all models. Key Changes B-series mild hybrid (B3, B4, B5, B6) replaces T-series across XC40, XC60, XC90, S60, V60, S90, V90 from 2020 Belt-integrated Starter Generator (BSG) with 48V system enables engine-off coasting, improved start-stop, and energy recuperation Engine codes shift to B420T-series (B420T4 = B4, B420T2/T10 = B5) - all share 2.0L I4 Drive-E architecture with mild hybrid overlay Diesel range progressively withdrawn - D4 diesel exits XC40, XC60, and S60 between 2020–2022 T8 Recharge continues alongside B-series - XC90, XC60, V60, S90 retain plug-in hybrid as premium option above mild hybrid range Main Engines B420T4 / B420T6 - B4 2.0L I4 48V MHEV petrol 197hp (Volvo XC40, XC60, S60, V60, XC90) B420T2 / B420T10 - B5 2.0L I4 48V MHEV petrol 250hp (Volvo XC60, XC90, S60 AWD) B420T (high output) - B6 2.0L I4 48V MHEV petrol 300hp (Volvo XC90, XC60, S60 R-Design) B420T8 - B3 2.0L I4 48V MHEV petrol 163hp (Volvo XC40 entry, V40 replacement) B4204T34/T35/T39 - T8 Recharge PHEV 318–333hp (Volvo XC90, XC60, S90, V60 Recharge) Popular Models Volvo XC60 B5 AWD Volvo XC90 B5/B6 AWD Volvo XC40 B4/B3 Volvo S60 B5 AWD Volvo V60 B4/B5 Volvo XC90/XC60 T8 Recharge Known For B-series mild hybrid BSG integration delivers seamless engine-off coasting in UK motorway conditions - real-world fuel savings of 8–12% reported by Volvo fleet operators compared with equivalent T-series predecessors BSG belt-starter-generator is an additional failure point - belt and BSG unit condition should be confirmed at 60,000-mile service; early replacement complaints have emerged on high-mileage B5 XC60 examples EGR cooler sealing concern on Drive-E base engine carries over into B-series architecture - same two-chamber cooler design; UK specialists note the issue is present on B4 and B5 units as well as the outgoing D4 diesels Diesel removal from XC40 and XC60 creates a growing secondary market demand for reconditioned D4204T units from 2016–2020 cars - supply of quality rebuilt Drive-E diesels is increasingly important for independent Volvo workshops [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: B420T4, B420T5, B420T6, B420T2, B420T8, B420T10, B4204T34, B4204T35, B4204T39, B4204T48
years.cta: Find 2020–2022 Volvo B4/B5/B6 mild hybrid replacement engines from UK specialists

years.preview: EX-Series Electric, Recharge PHEV Expansion & Final Combustion Range 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Volvo's current period is defined by the rapid expansion of the EX electric family - EX30, EX40, EX90 - alongside continued B-series mild hybrid production and the T8 Recharge PHEV as the performance option, making this the most combustion-diverse and simultaneously most electrified Volvo lineup in the brand's history. Key Changes EX30 (2023) - fully electric, 272–428hp dual-motor; Volvo's entry-level EV on CMA platform EX40 (2024) - electric version of XC40 on CMA platform; replaces XC40 Recharge Pure Electric branding EX90 (2024) - flagship electric SUV replacing XC90 in electric form; faq.h2: Volvo Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Volvo owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Volvo engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A typical Volvo engine replacement costs between £1,800 and £4,500 for a reconditioned unit with supply and fitting, depending on the model. A 2.0‑litre Drive‑E D4 diesel or T5 petrol usually lands between £2,500 and £3,800, while a 2.4‑litre D5 five‑cylinder costs £2,200 to £3,500. Used engines start around £1,200–£2,000, but a fully rebuilt unit with upgraded timing components and a 12‑month warranty is nearly always better value for long‑term ownership. Add £450–£800 for fitting by a UK Volvo specialist.
items.cta: Compare Volvo engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Volvo engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors drive the price: engine size and family (Drive‑E four‑cylinder vs D5 five‑cylinder vs T8 hybrid), engine condition (used, reconditioned, or rebuilt), vehicle age and complexity (XC90 takes longer than V40), and whether you choose supply‑only or supply‑and‑fit. Labour rates also vary by location – London and the South East can be 20–30% higher than the Midlands or North. Our comparison tool shows transparent, like‑for‑like quotes from Volvo specialists across the UK so you can see exactly what affects your final price.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Volvo engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Volvo?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and bodywork are solid. A rebuilt Volvo engine with upgraded components (e.g., revised timing chain guides on Drive‑E units, uprated piston rings on early T5 petrols) often outlasts the original factory engine. For a Volvo worth £3,000–£10,000, replacement is almost always more cost‑effective than scrapping the car and buying a replacement vehicle of unknown history. If your Volvo is a high‑mileage V70 D5 or XC90 with otherwise sound underpinnings, a rebuilt engine is a smart, long‑term investment.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Volvo engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: What's included in a Volvo engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit service includes sourcing the correct reconditioned or rebuilt engine for your specific Volvo model and engine code, removing the failed unit, installing the replacement with all new gaskets, seals, fluids, and filters, and then road testing the vehicle. It also includes a workmanship warranty (typically 12 months), disposal of the old engine, and a thorough health check of associated components like the cooling system and turbo oil feed lines. Fitting costs typically add £450–£800 depending on the model and specialist location.
items.cta: Compare Volvo engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a Volvo engine replacement take?
items.answer: For most common Volvos – V60, XC60, S60, V40, V70 – a like‑for‑like engine swap takes 3 to 5 working days. More complex models like the XC90 (especially T8 Twin Engine PHEV) can take 5 to 7 days due to the additional hybrid system integration and tight engine bays. The biggest variable is engine availability: our network of specialists stocks commonly replaced codes including D4204T, B4204T, D5244T, and B5254T, which significantly speeds up turnaround. Always ask your chosen specialist for a confirmed lead time before authorising work.
items.cta: Get quotes from Volvo engine specialists with clear lead times

items.question: Do reconditioned Volvo engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every reconditioned or rebuilt Volvo engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many specialists also offer extended 24‑month coverage on fully rebuilt units with new timing chains, piston rings, bearings, and oil pumps. The warranty typically covers major internal components: crankshaft, conrods, pistons, cylinder head, valves, camshafts, timing chain, and oil pump. Always confirm exact terms – including whether labour is covered – directly with your chosen supplier before purchase.
items.cta: Compare Volvo engine warranty options from UK specialists

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Volvo engine?
items.answer: A used engine is cheaper upfront (£1,200–£2,000) and makes sense as a temporary fix or if you plan to sell the car within 12–18 months. However, you inherit the original weak points – early Drive‑E timing chain wear, D5 turbo actuator age, or five‑cylinder PCV clogging. A reconditioned engine costs more (£2,500–£4,500) but includes new piston rings, bearings, seals, and upgraded components that address the original failure points, plus a 12‑24 month warranty. For a Volvo you intend to keep, a reconditioned unit with known upgrades is nearly always the better long‑term value.

items.question: What are the most common Volvo engine failures?
items.answer: The most common failures driving Volvo engine replacement are: 1) early Drive‑E timing chain guide wear (D4204T/B4204T, 2014‑2018) – chain skip bends valves; 2) D5 turbo actuator seizure (D5244T, 2008‑2015) – limp mode, turbo overspeed; 3) DPF/EGR clogging on diesel models used for short journeys – leads to backpressure damage and ring failure; 4) 5‑cylinder crankshaft harmonic balancer separation (B5244S/B5254T/D5244T) – can snap the timing belt; 5) T8 PHEV high‑voltage inverter failure (XC90/XC60/S90) – disables hybrid system and often requires ERAD replacement. These failures nearly always lead to replacement rather than repair once catastrophic damage occurs.
items.cta: Compare replacement engines for common Volvo failure points

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Volvo engine?
items.answer: Always compare at least three quotes – prices for the same engine code can vary by £1,000+ between specialists. For tight budgets, a used engine from a breaker offers the lowest entry price (£1,200–£2,000), but for long‑term ownership, invest in a reconditioned unit with upgraded failure‑point components. Ask for supply‑only if you have a trusted local garage – this saves the specialist's fitting markup. Crucially, check exactly what's included: delivery, VAT, old unit collection, gaskets, oil, and the warranty terms. Our comparison tool shows all these line items transparently so you can spot the genuine best deal.
